content.text: When we compare different quantities, we often notice patterns in how they change. For example, when images are resized, some maintain their appearance while others appear distorted. This depends on whether the changes to their dimensions occur by the same factor.

content.title: Example: Image Dimensions
content.content:
Consider rectangles with different dimensions:
Image A: Width 60mm, Height 40mm
Image C: Width 30mm, Height 20mm
Image D: Width 90mm, Height 60mm

When comparing A and C:
- Width changes by factor: 30/60 = 1/2
- Height changes by factor: 20/40 = 1/2
- Both change by same factor → Images A and C look similar

When comparing A and B (Width 40mm, Height 20mm):
- Width changes by factor: 40/60 = 2/3
- Height changes by factor: 20/40 = 1/2
- Different factors → Images look different (distorted)

content.title: Key Concept: Proportional Change
content.text: Two quantities change proportionally when they both change by the same factor through multiplication. Changing by different amounts (through subtraction) does not maintain proportionality.

A ratio is a way to compare two quantities using the notation a:b. It expresses a relationship where for every 'a' units of the first quantity, there are 'b' units of the second quantity. The numbers a and b are called the terms of the ratio.

content.title: Ratio Definition
content.text: A ratio a:b compares two quantities. It states: for every a units of the first quantity, there are b units of the second quantity. The terms 'a' and 'b' are the numbers in the ratio.

content.title: Example: Lemonade Ratio
content.content:
Kesang's lemonade recipe:
- 6 glasses of lemonade : 10 spoons of sugar
This ratio (6:10) means: for every 6 glasses of lemonade, use 10 spoons of sugar to maintain consistent sweetness.

content.text: Ratios can be compared by checking if they change by the same factor. When two ratios have the same proportional relationship, we say they are proportional ratios.
To reduce a ratio to its simplest form, divide both terms by their Highest Common Factor (HCF). This gives the most basic representation of the ratio.

content.title: Example: Simplifying Ratios
content.content:
Ratio 60:40
HCF(60,40) = 20
Simplified ratio = 60÷20 : 40÷20 = 3:2

Ratio 90:60
HCF(90,60) = 30
Simplified ratio = 90÷30 : 60÷30 = 3:2

Since both simplify to 3:2, they are proportional ratios.

content.title: Proportional Ratios Symbol
content.text:
When two ratios are proportional, we use the symbol '::' to indicate this.
For example: 60:40 :: 90:60 means these ratios are proportional.
Both ratios in simplest form equal 3:2.
To solve problems involving proportions, identify the proportional relationship and use the fact that all corresponding terms change by the same factor.

content.title: Example 1: Checking if Ratios are Proportional
content.content:
Are ratios 3:4 and 72:96 proportional?

Ratio 3:4 is already in simplest form.

For ratio 72:96:
HCF(72,96) = 24
Simplified: 72÷24 : 96÷24 = 3:4

Both simplify to 3:4, so they ARE proportional.
We write: 3:4 :: 72:96

content.title: Example 2: Using Proportional Reasoning
content.content:
Problem: For 6 glasses of lemonade, use 10 spoons of sugar. How much sugar for 18 glasses?

Set up proportion: 6:10 :: 18:?

Find the scaling factor:
First term: 18÷6 = 3

Apply same factor to second term:
Second term: 10 × 3 = 30

Answer: Use 30 spoons of sugar for 18 glasses.
Verification: 6:10 :: 18:30 (both simplify to 3:5)

content.title: Example 3: Real-World Application
content.content:
Nitin's wall: 60 ft length, 3 bags cement → Ratio 60:3 = 20:1
Hari's wall: 40 ft length, 2 bags cement → Ratio 40:2 = 20:1

Both ratios are proportional (both equal 20:1), so both walls are equally strong. The cement was used proportionally to wall length.

topics.topic_name: Adding or Subtracting from Ratio Terms

content.text: An important observation: when we add or subtract the same number from the terms of a ratio, the ratio changes and is NOT necessarily proportional to the original ratio.

content.title: Example: Age Ratio Change
content.content:
When Neelima is 3 years old, her mother is 30 years old.
Ratio of Neelima:Mother = 3:30 = 1:10 (simplest form)

After 9 years:
Neelima's age: 3 + 9 = 12
Mother's age: 30 + 9 = 39
New ratio: 12:39 = 4:13 (simplest form)

The ratios 1:10 and 4:13 are NOT proportional.
Addition changes the ratio in a non-proportional way.

content.title: Key Insight
content.text: Proportional relationships are maintained through multiplication (same factor applied to all terms), not through addition or subtraction (fixed amounts added/subtracted).
In practical applications like mixing beverages, ratios determine the strength or intensity of the final product. By comparing ratios, we can determine which mixture is stronger or weaker.

content.title: Example: Coffee Strength Comparison
content.content:
Regular coffee: 15 mL decoction : 35 mL milk = 15:35
Stronger coffee: 20 mL decoction : 30 mL milk = 20:30
Lighter coffee: 10 mL decoction : 40 mL milk = 10:40

Simplify each:
Regular: 15:35 = 3:7 (decoction to milk ratio)
Stronger: 20:30 = 2:3 (more decoction relative to milk)
Lighter: 10:40 = 1:4 (less decoction relative to milk)

Comparison: 1:4 < 3:7 < 2:3
So: Lighter < Regular < Stronger

content.text: The Rule of Three is an ancient method (credited to Aryabhata from 199 CE) for solving problems where three quantities are known and we must find a fourth proportional quantity. The principle is that if a:b :: c:d, then ad = bc (cross multiplication).

content.title: Cross Multiplication Property
content.text:
For proportional ratios a:b :: c:d:
a × d = b × c

This property allows us to find the unknown term:
If a:b :: c:x, then x = (b × c) / a

content.title: Example: School Rice Distribution
content.content:
Problem: For 120 students, cook makes 15 kg rice. On a rainy day, only 80 students come. How much rice should be made?

Set up: 120:15 :: 80:x

Using cross multiplication:
120 × x = 15 × 80
x = (15 × 80) / 120
x = 1200 / 120
x = 10 kg

Alternative: Find scaling factor:
80/120 = 2/3
Rice: 15 × (2/3) = 10 kg

content.title: Example: Distance and Time
content.content:
Problem: Car travels 90 km in 150 minutes. How far in 4 hours (240 minutes)?

Note: Convert units first - 4 hours = 240 minutes

Set up: 150:90 :: 240:x

Cross multiply:
150 × x = 90 × 240
x = (90 × 240) / 150
x = 21600 / 150
x = 144 km

content.text: When two quantities change by the same factor, they are proportional. Two ratios are proportional if their cross products are equal: a:b and c:d are proportional if a×d = b×c.

content.title: Example: Idli Batter Proportions
content.content:
Standard recipe: 2 cups rice : 1 cup urad dal

Viswanath: 6 cups rice : 3 cups urad dal = 6:3
Puneet: 4 cups rice : 2 cups urad dal = 4:2

Are they proportional?
Check cross multiplication:
6 × 2 = 12
3 × 4 = 12

Since 6×2 = 3×4, the ratios ARE proportional.
Both should taste the same if cooked identically.

content.title: Proportionality Conditions
content.text:
Two ratios a:b and c:d are proportional if:
1. a×d = b×c (cross multiplication)
2. a/c = b/d (quotient equality)
3. They reduce to the same simplest form

Maps use ratios to represent real-world distances on a smaller scale. A Representative Fraction (RF) expresses the ratio between a distance on the map and the corresponding actual distance on the ground.

content.title: Representative Fraction (RF)
content.text:
RF shows the scale of a map. For example:
RF 1:60,00,000 means 1 cm on map = 60,00,000 cm on ground

To convert:
60,00,000 cm = 600,000 m = 600 km

So: 1 cm on map represents 600 km on ground.

content.title: Example: Map Distance Calculation
content.content:
Map scale: 1 cm = 100 km (or RF 1:1,00,00,000)

If distance between two cities on map = 5 cm
Actual distance = 5 × 100 = 500 km

Using proportion:
1:100 :: 5:x
x = 5 × 100 = 500 km

content.title: Important Note
content.text: RF expresses geographical distance (straight line), not road distance. Actual travel distance may be longer due to roads not being straight.
Ratios can have more than two terms when comparing multiple quantities. For multiple-term ratios to be proportional, each corresponding term must change by the same factor.

content.title: Example: Spice Mix Powder
content.content:
Viswanath's recipe: 8:4:2:1 (coriander:chillies:toor dal:fenugreek)

Puneet has only 2 chillies (half of 4), so scale all ingredients by 1/2:
8×(1/2) : 4×(1/2) : 2×(1/2) : 1×(1/2)
= 4:2:1:0.5

These ratios are proportional: 8:4:2:1 :: 4:2:1:0.5

content.title: Multiple-Term Proportionality
content.text:
For ratios a:b:c:d and p:q:r:s to be proportional:
a/p = b/q = c/r = d/s = k (same scaling factor)

topics.topic_name: 3.4 Dividing a Whole in a Given Ratio

content.text: To divide a quantity in a specific ratio, sum all ratio terms, then multiply the quantity by each term's fraction of the total.

content.title: Division Formula
content.text:
To divide quantity x in ratio a:b:c:
First part = x × (a/(a+b+c))
Second part = x × (b/(a+b+c))
Third part = x × (c/(a+b+c))

content.title: Example: Paint Mixture
content.content:
Need 50 mL purple paint in ratio Red:Blue:White = 2:3:5

Total parts = 2+3+5 = 10

Red paint = 50 × (2/10) = 50 × 0.2 = 10 mL
Blue paint = 50 × (3/10) = 50 × 0.3 = 15 mL
White paint = 50 × (5/10) = 50 × 0.5 = 25 mL

Verification: 10 + 15 + 25 = 50 mL ✓

content.title: Example: Triangle Angles
content.content:
Construct triangle with angles in ratio 1:3:5

Sum of angles in triangle = 180°
Total ratio parts = 1+3+5 = 9

Angle A = 180° × (1/9) = 20°
Angle B = 180° × (3/9) = 60°
Angle C = 180° × (5/9) = 100°

Verification: 20° + 60° + 100° = 180° ✓

content.text: Pie charts visually represent proportions of a whole using angles. Since a circle has 360°, proportions are scaled to angles: Angle = (Part/Whole) × 360°.

content.title: Pie Chart Construction Steps
content.text:
1. Express each part as a fraction of the whole
2. Multiply each fraction by 360° to get the angle
3. Draw a circle and mark radii at calculated angles
4. Label and color each sector proportionally
Two quantities are inversely proportional when one increases as the other decreases by the same factor. The product of inversely proportional quantities remains constant: x × y = k.

content.title: Inverse Proportion Definition
content.text:
If x and y are inversely proportional:
- When x increases by factor n, y decreases by factor 1/n
- Their product is constant: x × y = k
- Formula: x₁ × y₁ = x₂ × y₂ = constant

content.title: Example: Speed and Time Relationship
content.content:
From Lucknow to Kanpur (90 km):
Walking (5 km/h) → 18 hours
Bicycle (15 km/h) → 6 hours
Motorcycle (30 km/h) → 3 hours
Car (60 km/h) → 1.5 hours

Notice: Speed × Time = constant
5 × 18 = 90
15 × 6 = 90
30 × 3 = 90
60 × 1.5 = 90

As speed increases 3× (5 to 15), time decreases 1/3 (18 to 6)

content.title: Example: Workers and Days
content.content:
Problem: 20 workers complete road in 4 days. How long for 10 workers?

Using inverse proportion:
20 × 4 = 10 × x
80 = 10x
x = 8 days

When workers halved (20→10), days doubled (4→8)
Product remains constant: 20×4 = 10×8 = 80

content.title: Example: Food Provisions
content.content:
Problem: Food feeds 80 students for 15 days. If 100 students join, how long will food last?

Using inverse proportion:
80 × 15 = 100 × x
1200 = 100x
x = 12 days

Product (total student-days of food) remains constant: 80×15 = 100×12 = 1200

content.text: Direct proportions (like distance and time at constant speed) differ from inverse proportions. In direct proportion, both quantities change in the same direction. In inverse proportion, they change in opposite directions.

content.title: Key Differences
content.text:
DIRECT PROPORTION:
- Both quantities change in same direction
- When one increases, other increases
- Formula: y = k × x (product not constant)
- Examples: Distance & time at constant speed, Cost & quantity

INVERSE PROPORTION:
- Quantities change in opposite directions
- When one increases, other decreases
- Formula: x × y = k (product is constant)
- Examples: Speed & time, Number of workers & days needed

content.title: Example: Identifying Proportions
content.content:
1. Taps filling tank vs Time: INVERSE (more taps → less time)
2. Number of painters vs Days for wall: INVERSE (more painters → fewer days)
3. Distance car travels vs Petrol in tank: DIRECT (more petrol → more distance)
4. Speed of cyclist vs Time for fixed route: INVERSE (faster speed → less time)
5. Length of cloth vs Price (fixed rate): DIRECT (more cloth → higher price)
6. Number of book pages vs Time to read: Direct or Inverse depends on context

glossary.definition: A comparison of two quantities using the notation a:b, meaning for every 'a' units of the first quantity, there are 'b' units of the second quantity.
The numbers in a ratio. In the ratio a:b, both 'a' and 'b' are terms of the ratio.
Two ratios are proportional when their terms change by the same factor. They have the same relationship and are denoted using the symbol '::'.
A ratio reduced by dividing both terms by their Highest Common Factor (HCF), giving the most basic representation.
A method to check if two ratios are proportional or to solve for an unknown term. For a:b :: c:d, we have a×d = b×c.
An ancient mathematical method for solving proportionality problems where three quantities are known and a fourth is to be found.
A ratio shown on maps indicating the scale, such as 1:60,00,000, meaning 1 unit on map represents 60,00,000 units on ground.
A ratio with more than two terms, such as a:b:c:d, used when comparing multiple quantities that maintain proportional relationships.
A relationship between two quantities where one increases as the other decreases by the same factor, with their product remaining constant (x × y = k).
A relationship between two quantities where both change in the same direction by the same factor.
In inverse proportions, the fixed product value: x × y = k. In direct proportions, the scaling factor relating the quantities.
The factor by which all terms of a ratio are multiplied or divided to maintain proportional relationships.
A change achieved through multiplication by the same factor applied to all related quantities.
A circular graph divided into sectors, where each sector's angle is proportional to the quantity it represents.
The greatest number that divides evenly into all terms of a ratio, used to reduce the ratio to its simplest form.
The straight-line distance between two points on a map, as opposed to actual road distance which may be longer.
A relationship where two or more quantities change by the same factor, maintaining a consistent ratio between them.

summary_points: A ratio a:b compares two quantities, meaning for every 'a' units of the first quantity, there are 'b' units of the second. Ratios are proportional when their terms change by the same factor.
Two ratios are proportional if they reduce to the same simplest form or if their cross products are equal: a:b :: c:d when a×d = b×c.
To reduce a ratio to simplest form, divide both terms by their Highest Common Factor (HCF). This gives the most basic representation maintaining the same relationship.
Proportional change occurs through multiplication (same factor applied to all terms), not through addition or subtraction of fixed amounts. Adding/subtracting changes the ratio non-proportionally.
The Rule of Three (Trairasika) solves proportionality problems using cross multiplication. When a:b :: c:x, then x = (b×c)/a.
Representative Fractions on maps show scale: RF 1:60,00,000 means 1 cm on map = 60,00,000 cm on ground (or 600 km). Maps represent geographical distance, not road distance.
Multiple-term ratios (a:b:c:d) are proportional when all corresponding terms change by the same factor. For proportional multiple-term ratios: a/p = b/q = c/r = d/s.
To divide a quantity x in ratio a:b:c, calculate each part as: x × (term/(sum of all terms)). The parts sum to x and maintain the specified ratio.
Pie charts represent proportions using angles. Each sector's angle = (part value/total value) × 360°. The angles sum to 360°, representing the complete whole.
Inverse proportions show opposite directional changes: when one quantity increases by factor n, the other decreases by factor 1/n. Their product remains constant: x × y = k.
In inverse proportions, if x₁ and y₁ are first pair and x₂ and y₂ are second pair, then x₁×y₁ = x₂×y₂. Examples: speed-time, workers-days, number of taps-time to fill.
Direct proportions show same-directional changes: both quantities increase or decrease together. Examples: distance-time at constant speed, cost-quantity at fixed rate.
To distinguish proportions: identify if quantities change in same direction (direct) or opposite directions (inverse). Check using constant product (inverse) or constant ratio (direct).
Proportional reasoning applies to real-world problems like recipe scaling, mixture strength comparison, resource distribution, and time management.
Practical applications of proportions include: map reading, cement mixing (construction ratios), paint blending, food distribution, and scheduling (workers and completion time).
